1: The Sinai covenant is the central structuring event of the OT: it establishes the constitutional framework of Israel's relationship with YHWH. Its suzerainty-treaty structure (parallel to Hittite treaties: preamble, historical prologue, stipulations, blessings/curses, witnesses) places YHWH as the great king and Israel as his vassal. The Decalogue (Exodus 20:1-17) is the covenant's summary stipulations. The Book of the Covenant (Exodus 20:22-23:33) elaborates case law. The covenant-inauguration ceremony (Exodus 24:1-11) seals the relationship with blood and a covenant meal. Hebrews 12:18-24 contrasts the terrors of Sinai with the joy of Zion — the old covenant's thunders and fire point forward to the new covenant's completed mediation in Christ.
2: They had journeyed from Rephidim (possibly the Wadi Feiran area in the Sinai) and arrived in the Sinai wilderness. The traditional identification of Mount Sinai is Jebel Musa in the southern Sinai Peninsula (ca. 2,285m), the location venerated since the early Byzantine period and confirmed by St. Catherine's Monastery. An alternative identification places Sinai at Jebel al-Lawz in northwest Saudi Arabia. Israel camped at the foot of the mountain — the camp and the mountain are two zones of the encounter, with the mountain as the vertical axis connecting earth to the divine presence above.
3: Moses went up to God, and YHWH called to him from the mountain. In ANE cosmology, mountains were the dwelling places of the gods — the high place where heaven and earth meet. Ugaritic literature describes Baal dwelling on Mount Zaphon; Sumerian texts locate divine temples as mountain replicas (ziggurats as artificial sacred mountains). YHWH's use of a mountain as the site of covenant communication operates within this recognizable religious geography while surpassing it: YHWH is not confined to the mountain but descends to it (Exodus 19:18, 20). The covenant announced in Exodus 19:3-6 follows the structure of ANE suzerain-vassal treaties (preamble, historical prologue, stipulations, blessings/curses).
4: The eagle's wing metaphor — 'I carried you on eagles' wings and brought you to myself' — is developed more fully in Deuteronomy 32:10-11, where YHWH is like an eagle stirring its nest and bearing its young on its wings. The eagle carries its eaglets on its back to protect them from attack from below; if an arrow is shot at the young, it strikes the parent first. The metaphor claims that the Exodus was not Israel's achievement or even Moses' — it was YHWH's sovereign act of parental rescue. 'To myself' (elay): the goal of the Exodus was not Canaan but YHWH himself. The land is the means; the relationship is the end.
5: Segullah — 'treasured possession' (H5459, appears only 8x in OT). In the ancient world, a king's segullah was his private treasury, his personally owned moveable wealth — not the national treasury but his personal reserve. The term appears in ANE royal inscriptions for a king's chosen people or city. YHWH claims Israel as his personal treasury among all the peoples — not because Israel is large or powerful but by covenantal election. The conditional structure ('if you truly obey my voice and keep my covenant') places this identity within the suzerain-vassal treaty form: the vassal's privileged status depends on fidelity to the great king's terms.
6: Mamlekhet kohanim ve-goy qadosh — 'kingdom of priests and a holy nation.' Israel's national vocation is twofold: priestly (to mediate between YHWH and the nations) and holy (set apart, distinctive). The priestly role is corporate and international: as Aaron mediates between YHWH and Israel, so Israel mediates between YHWH and all nations. The term goy qadosh (holy nation) applies the cultic word for consecrated persons (qadosh) to the whole nation — all Israel, not just the Levites, is constituted as a priestly people. 1 Pet 2:9 applies this verse directly to the church in Christ: 'a royal priesthood, a holy nation.' The NT claim is that in Christ the vocation of Israel is extended to all who are grafted in.
7: Moses convened the elders of Israel and set before them all these words — the covenant is presented through representative governance structures. The elders (zeqenim) are the tribal heads who constitute Israel's pre-monarchic political body. The covenant is communal: Moses does not present the terms to individuals but to the assembled representatives of the whole people. The covenant is accepted on behalf of the entire community, with implications that bind all generations.
8: 'All that YHWH has spoken we will do' (kol asher diber YHWH na'aseh) — the covenant ratification speech. The entire people respond together (yachdav). This communal acceptance formula will be formalized in blood at Ex Exodus 24:3-8 ('the blood of the covenant'). Significantly, the people commit to 'do' the covenant before they have heard all its detailed terms — the mishpatim (Ex 21-23) come after this acceptance, not before it. The sequence mirrors the NT structure: covenant relationship precedes law-keeping; grace is the ground, obedience the response.
9: YHWH will come to Moses in a thick cloud (anan av) so the people can hear him speak with Moses — the cloud serves two functions: it conceals the divine form (no one can see YHWH and live, Exodus 33:20) while transmitting divine speech. The auditory theophany is specifically designed to validate Moses' prophetic authority in the eyes of the people: they hear YHWH speaking with Moses, which grounds their trust in subsequent Mosaic communication. The cloud is the technology of divine mediation — presence without visibility, communication without direct exposure.
10: YHWH instructs Moses to consecrate the people — wash their clothes and be ready by the third day. The three-day preparation period creates liturgical anticipation and ritual purity. Washing clothes in the ANE was a standard purification rite before sacred events; the Levitical purity laws (which develop this much further) build on this practice. 'The third day' carries covenant weight throughout the OT: Abraham sees the mountain on the third day at the Aqedah (Genesis 22:4); Jonah is in the fish three days; and the resurrection of the dead connects explicitly to this theophanic pattern (Hosea 6:2; 1 Corinthians 15:4).
11: On the third day YHWH will come down on Mount Sinai in the sight of all the people. The descent of YHWH to Sinai is described with the same verb (yarad = came down) used in 3:8 for the divine descent to deliver Israel. YHWH does not merely summon Israel upward but comes down to them — the pattern of divine condescension that characterizes the Exodus narrative as a whole. The 'sight of all the people' is the democratic dimension of the theophany: the covenant is not a private mystical experience for Moses but a public event witnessed by the entire community.
12: Barriers are set around the mountain — anyone who touches the mountain must be put to death. The spatial holiness of Sinai anticipates the graduated access structure of the tabernacle: the outer court, holy place, and holy of holies correspond to the people's camp, the foot of the mountain, and the summit. Physical boundaries enforce theological realities: divine holiness is not safely approached without proper mediation. The lethal boundary teaches what the tabernacle will architecturally encode: nearness to the holy requires preparation, permission, and the right mediator.
13: The method of execution for violating the boundary: stoning or shooting with arrows — at a distance, because even the executioner must not touch the boundary-violator (now ritually contaminated by touching the sacred zone without permission). When the long trumpet blast sounds, the people may go up. The trumpet (yovel, the ram's horn) signals the end of the dangerous divine approach and the beginning of sanctioned access. The Jubilee year is announced by the same instrument (Leviticus 25:9-10) — the trumpet of liberation and the trumpet of access are the same sound.
14: Moses went down from the mountain to the people and consecrated them — he is the active agent of consecration, the mediator who prepares the people for the divine encounter. The people washed their clothes — in ancient Israelite practice, linen garments were associated with purity (cf. the white linen of the priests), while clothes contaminated by ordinary life required washing before any cultic event. The physical act of washing is not merely hygienic but symbolic: the outer act expresses an inner reorientation toward YHWH.
15: 'Be ready for the third day; do not approach a woman.' The requirement of sexual abstinence before a major cultic event is attested in multiple ANE contexts. It is not that sexuality is sinful but that the total dedication of the person to the moment of divine encounter requires temporary withdrawal from even lawful pleasures. Paul applies a similar principle in 1 Corinthians 7:5 — spouses may agree to temporary abstinence for prayer. The logic is concentration, not condemnation: the meeting with YHWH at Sinai requires the full attention of the whole self.
16: On the morning of the third day — thunder, lightning, a thick cloud over the mountain, and the sound of a very loud trumpet (shofar). All the people in the camp trembled. The Sinai theophany employs the standard ANE storm-god imagery: thunder (qolot = voices/thunders), lightning (beraqim), thick cloud, fire, earthquake. The Baal cycle of Ugarit describes Baal as the storm-deity who speaks in thunder and lightning. YHWH appropriates this imagery while emptying it of Baal's content — this is not Baal's storm but YHWH's self-disclosure, which is why the theophany must be distinguished from all other storm experiences. The shofar (ram's horn) will later announce the Jubilee, the New Year, and royal accessions — the same instrument marks cosmic-covenantal transitions.
17: Moses brought the people out of the camp to meet God — the pilgrimage from the camp (ordinary space) to the mountain (sacred space) is the structural movement of Israelite worship. The people take up their stations at the foot of the mountain. The Hebrew lemrat haElohim = 'to meet/confront God' — the word liqrat is used for the formal procession of one party to meet another (cf. Moses meeting his father-in-law in Exodus 18:7, Abraham meeting the kings in Genesis 14:17). The encounter with YHWH at Sinai is a formal, procedural event — structured, not accidental.
18: Sinai was covered with smoke because YHWH descended on it in fire — the fire and smoke column reactivates the imagery of the Exodus march (Ex Exodus 13:21-22, the pillar of fire and cloud). The comparison to a kiln (kibshan) may evoke the smoke from Egypt's mudbrick kilns — the fire that oppressed Israel (Ex Exodus 1:14, the brick-kilns) becomes the fire of divine presence. The whole mountain trembled greatly (yecherad kol hahar meod) — the seismic response to divine approach is characteristic of OT theophanies (cf. Psalms 68:7-8: 'the earth trembled at the presence of God, the Sinai at the presence of God').
19: The sound of the trumpet grew louder and louder — the escalating intensity of the theophany communicates the approach of the divine: it is not static but dynamic. Moses spoke (yedaber) and God answered him in thunder (baqol — literally 'in a voice/thunder'). The divine speech in thunder is not merely auditory accompaniment but the vehicle of divine communication — the thunder is the divine voice before the articulate words of the Decalogue. Ps 29 meditates on this: 'the voice of YHWH is over the waters... the voice of YHWH breaks the cedars... the voice of YHWH strikes with flashes of lightning.'
20: YHWH descended to the top of Mount Sinai and called Moses to the top — the summit meeting between YHWH (descending from above) and Moses (ascending from below) defines the mediatorial role. Moses ascends and descends multiple times in Ex 19-34; each movement represents a stage in the covenant-making process. The vertical axis of the mountain — divine above, people below, Moses as mediator between — is the spatial expression of the prophetic office. The NT equivalent is Christ as the mediator who descends from the Father and ascends back to him (John 3:13; Exodus 20:17).
21: YHWH sends Moses back down to warn the people not to break through (yehershu) to see YHWH — 'lest many of them die.' The warning anticipates the later death of Nadab and Abihu (Leviticus 10:1-2), Uzzah's death when he touched the ark (2 Samuel 6:6-7), and the deaths in Beth-shemesh when they looked into the ark (1 Samuel 6:19). Unauthorized approach to the divine presence is not treated as merely offensive but as physically lethal. This is not divine cruelty but the nature of absolute holiness meeting finite, unmediated sinfulness. The tabernacle system, with its graduated access and prescribed atonement, is the divinely provided solution to the problem of human proximity to the holy.
22: Even the priests who come near to YHWH must consecrate themselves — the warning is leveled specifically at those who have designated access. The priests have an institutional right of approach, but even that right requires constant preparation. The principle: rank and title do not substitute for consecration. The NT equivalent is the warning to those who approach the Lord's Supper unworthily (1 Corinthians 11:27-31) — institutional participation does not guarantee safe approach; the attitude of the heart must correspond to what is received.
23: Moses argues back: the people cannot come up because you yourself commanded barriers around the mountain. The dialogue reveals the frank, negotiating character of the Mosaic relationship with YHWH — Moses is not merely a passive recipient of divine commands but an active mediator who clarifies, argues, and intercedes. This pattern (Moses interceding, debating, reminding YHWH of his own commitments) recurs throughout Ex 32-34 and shapes the OT prophetic tradition of covenantal advocacy before the divine throne.
24: Only Aaron may come up with Moses — but the priests and people must not break through. The three levels of access correspond to the three zones of the tabernacle that will be described in Ex 25-27: people (outer court), priests (holy place), Moses and Aaron (holy of holies). The Sinai theophany is the original template for the tabernacle's spatial theology: the mountain with its zones of access becomes the portable sanctuary with its zones of holiness. Both encode the same theological reality: graduated nearness to the divine presence requires graduated consecration.
25: Moses went down to the people and spoke to them — the closing verse of the preparation completes the pattern. The theophany of Exodus 20 follows immediately. The narrative structure of Exodus 19 (preparation, warning, boundary-setting, final descent) mirrors the liturgical preparation for the Levitical feasts: days of preparation precede the moment of divine encounter. The covenant people do not rush into the divine presence; they are prepared, purified, and positioned by a mediator before the encounter occurs.
